channel.title: Type-Mismatch on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Type-Mismatch on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Type Mismatch — Compile-Time Type Fix
item.description:
Type Mismatch — Compile-Time Type Fix

A type mismatch error occurs at compile time when Kotlin’s type system detects an incompatible type. This is a compile error, not a runtime exception, and must be fixed before the code can run.

Description

Kotlin has a strict type system that catches type errors during compilation. Type mismatch happens when you assign a value of one type to a variable of a different type without proper conversion.

item.title: [Solution] SQL Type Mismatch / Incorrect Value Error Fix
item.description:
SQL Type Mismatch / Incorrect Value Error Fix

This error occurs when a value doesn’t match the expected data type of the target column. The message reads: Incorrect datetime value, Incorrect ... value for column 'X', or Truncated incorrect ... value.

Description

SQL columns have strict data types. When inserting or updating, the database attempts to cast the provided value to the column’s type. If the cast fails — e.g., a string like 'abc' into a DATE column — the operation is rejected.

item.title: [Solution] Swift Error — Could Not Cast Value of Type
item.description:
Could Not Cast Value of Type

This error occurs when you force-cast (as!) an object to a type it doesn’t actually conform to. The runtime detects the mismatch and crashes.

Description

Swift provides as? (conditional cast) and as! (forced cast) for type casting. The forced cast as! will crash if the object isn’t of the target type. This commonly happens with heterogeneous collections, Any typed values, and decoded JSON data.

item.title: [Solution] Swift Error — JSON Type Mismatch
item.description:
JSON Type Mismatch

This error occurs when JSONDecoder finds a value in the JSON that doesn’t match the expected Swift type. For example, finding a string where the struct expects an Int.

Description

DecodingError.typeMismatch indicates the JSON has a value, but its type doesn’t match what the Swift model expects. This commonly happens when APIs return numbers as strings, booleans as integers, or when the JSON structure changes between API versions.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2322 — Type Not Assignable
item.description:
TS2322 — Type ‘X’ is not assignable to type ‘Y’

TS2322 occurs when you assign a value of one type to a variable, parameter, or return position that expects a different, incompatible type. TypeScript’s structural type system checks that the source type satisfies all requirements of the target type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Assigning a string to a number
let age: number = "twenty-five"; // TS2322: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'

// Cause 2: Missing required properties
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2322: Property 'age' is missing

// Cause 3: Assigning nullable to non-nullable
let name: string = null; // TS2322 if strictNullChecks is on

// Cause 4: Returning wrong type from function
function getLength(): number {
 return "hello"; // TS2322: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the correct type

// Wrong
let age: number = "twenty-five";

// Correct
let age: number = 25;

Fix 2: Provide all required properties

// Wrong
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };

// Correct
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };

Fix 3: Handle nullable values properly

// Wrong
let name: string = null;

// Correct — accept null
let name: string | null = null;

// Correct — use a default
let name: string = "unknown";

Fix 4: Use type assertions when you know the type

const input = document.getElementById("name") as HTMLInputElement;
input.value = "Alice";

Examples

// Example 1: Enum mismatch
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
let c: Color = Color.Red;

enum Direction { Up, Down }
let d: Direction = c; // TS2322: Type 'Color' is not assignable to type 'Direction'

// Example 2: Excess properties in object literal
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}
const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000, debug: true }; // TS2322

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable — similar but for function arguments.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — specific variant for missing properties.
  • TS2352: Conversion may be a mistake — type assertion issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2684 — The 'this' Context Type Is Not Assignable
item.description:
TS2684 — The ’this’ context of type ‘X’ is not assignable

TS2684 occurs when the actual this context in a function doesn’t match the declared this parameter type. This is stricter than TS2683 — TypeScript knows what this is, but it doesn’t match what the function expects.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: 'this' parameter type doesn't match the object
interface Logger {
 log: (msg: string) => void;
 prefix: string;
}
function doLog(this: Logger, msg: string) {
 console.log(this.prefix + msg);
}
const obj = { prefix: "[APP]", log: console.log };
doLog.call(obj, "hello"); // TS2684: 'this' context mismatch

// Cause 2: Method called on wrong object type
class Animal {
 speak() {}
}
class Robot {
 code() {}
}
const robot = new Robot();
const speak = Animal.prototype.speak;
speak.call(robot); // TS2684: Robot is not Animal

// Cause 3: Arrow function captures wrong 'this'
class Outer {
 value = 10;
 method() {
 const inner = {
 value: 20,
 getThis: () => this.value // Captures Outer's 'this', not inner's
 };
 }
}

// Cause 4: Calling method with incompatible receiver
interface HasId {
 id: number;
}
function getId(this: HasId) {
 return this.id;
}
getId.call({ name: "Alice" }); // TS2684: missing 'id'

Solutions

Fix 1: Ensure the object matches the ’this’ parameter type

interface Logger {
 log: (msg: string) => void;
 prefix: string;
}

// Wrong — obj doesn't fully match Logger
const obj = { prefix: "[APP]", log: console.log };

// Correct — ensure full type match
const obj: Logger = { prefix: "[APP]", log: console.log };
doLog.call(obj, "hello");

Fix 2: Use .bind() with the correct type

class Animal {
 speak() { return "speaking"; }
}
const animal = new Animal();
const speak = animal.speak.bind(animal);
speak(); // OK

Fix 3: Use arrow functions to capture the right ’this’

class Outer {
 value = 10;
 method() {
 // Arrow function captures Outer's this
 const getOuterValue = () => this.value;
 return getOuterValue();
 }
}

Fix 4: Match ’this’ parameter to actual context

interface HasId {
 id: number;
}

// Wrong — calling with object that doesn't have 'id'
getId.call({ name: "Alice" });

// Correct — pass object with required shape
getId.call({ id: 1, name: "Alice" });

Examples

// Example 1: React class component
class MyComponent {
 state = { count: 0 };
 handleClick() {
 this.setState({ count: this.state.count + 1 }); // 'this' must be MyComponent
 }
}

// Example 2: forEach callback with 'this' parameter
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.forEach(function (item) {
 console.log(this); // 'this' must match declared type
}, someObject);

Related Errors

  • TS2683: ’this’ implicitly has type ‘any’ — ’this’ has no type at all.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader assignability.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — function argument mismatches.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Type Assignment Error — Type Not Assignable to Type Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Type ‘X’ is not assignable to type ‘Y’

This error occurs when you assign a value of one type to a variable, parameter, or return position that expects a different, incompatible type. TypeScript’s structural type system checks that the source type satisfies all requirements of the target type.

Common Causes

  • Primitive type mismatch — assigning a string to a number variable
  • Missing required properties — an object literal is missing fields required by the target interface
  • Strict null violations — assigning null or undefined to a non-nullable type (with strictNullChecks)
  • Enum or literal type mismatch — using a value from one enum where another is expected

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Primitive mismatch
let age: number = "twenty-five"; // TS2322

// Fix: use the correct type
let age: number = 25;

// Cause 2: Missing properties
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2322

// Fix: provide all required fields
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };

// Cause 3: Null assigned to non-nullable
let name: string = null; // TS2322

// Fix: use union type
let name: string | null = null;

// Cause 4: Wrong function return type
function getLength(): number {
 return "hello"; // TS2322
}

// Fix: return the correct type
function getLength(): string {
 return "hello";
}

Examples

// Enum mismatch
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
enum Direction { Up, Down }
let c: Color = Color.Red;
let d: Direction = c; // TS2322: Color not assignable to Direction

// Excess properties in object literal
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}
const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000, debug: true }; // TS2322

// Function argument mismatch
function greet(name: string) {}
greet(42); // TS2322: number not assignable to string

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument of type not assignable — similar but for function arguments
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties
  • TS2352: Conversion may be a mistake — type assertion issues

item.title: [Solution] VBA Runtime Error 13 Type Mismatch Fix
item.description:
VBA Runtime Error 13: Type Mismatch Fix

This error occurs when you try to assign a value to a variable of an incompatible type, or pass an argument to a function that doesn’t match the expected type. The full message is: Run-time error '13': Type mismatch.

Description

VBA is strongly typed in many contexts. When a String is expected but a Variant containing an object is provided, or when a number is passed where a String is required, this error fires. It’s one of the most common VBA runtime errors.

item.title: [Solution] VBA Runtime Error 13 Type Mismatch Fix
item.description:
VBA Runtime Error 13: Type Mismatch Fix

This error occurs when you try to assign a value to a variable of an incompatible type, or pass an argument to a function that doesn’t match the expected type. The full message is: Run-time error '13': Type mismatch.

Description

VBA is strongly typed in many contexts. When a String is expected but a Variant containing an object is provided, or when a number is passed where a String is required, this error fires. It’s one of the most common VBA runtime errors.
